ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class; no specific pork-filled pau entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers steamed bread/bun category; no pork-filled pau-specific code exists. Approximate match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for pork pau or steamed filled bun in available terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium pork pau weighs approximately 80 g and is a common single-serving portion at Singapore hawker centres and dim sum stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Pau — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all core macros and secondary nutrients. Values preserved as-is from the HPB Foodo database entry for Pork Pau.

gi_source.title: Steamed wheat bun / baozi — analogous GI estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for steamed wheat-flour products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Singapore pork pau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: steamed white-flour bun (baozi/mantou class), which ranges 65–68 in peer-reviewed GI literature (Foster-Powell et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2002; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 65 × (31.12 × 0.80) / 100 = 65 × 24.9 / 100 ≈ 16.2. Serving size used: 80 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 369 mg/100 g combined with high sugar (11.08 g/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g), which promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit pork pau to one piece per occasion, avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as soy sauce dips, and choose lower-sodium filling variants where available, as the 369 mg sodium per 100 g combined with high sugar content drives both direct sodium load and insulin-mediated fluid retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate (31.12 g/100 g) from white wheat flour, elevated sugar (11.08 g/100 g), and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100 g) producing a medium-to-high postprandial glucose response (estimated GI 65)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict pork pau to half a piece or one small pau per sitting, pair it with non-starchy vegetables or a protein source to blunt the glycaemic response, and avoid consuming it alongside sugary beverages, given the estimated GI of 65 and glycaemic load of approximately 16 per standard serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.8 g/100 g from pork filling and shortening, sodium 369 mg/100 g, and trans fat 0.1 g/100 g from lard or partially hydrogenated shortening
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals managing cardiovascular disease, pork pau should be consumed occasionally and in single-piece portions, as the combination of saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), and sodium (369 mg/100 g) collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; choosing steamed over baked versions and requesting lean pork filling reduces the saturated fat burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork-derived arachidonic acid promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine synthesis, combined with high refined carbohydrate and sugar driving inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit pork pau frequency due to the pro-inflammatory combination of arachidonic acid from pork, high refined carbohydrate (31.12 g/100 g), sugar (11.08 g/100 g), and trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g); vegetable or tofu pau is a less pro-inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork pau (猪肉包; Zhu rou bao) is a steamed filled bun that is a staple of Singapore's hawker centres, dim sum stalls, and Chinese bakeries. The outer dough is made from refined white wheat flour leavened with yeast and enriched with sugar and lard or vegetable shortening, giving it a soft, pillowy, and slightly sweet texture. The filling consists of seasoned minced pork cooked with soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and sometimes five-spice powder, producing a savoury-sweet, juicy interior. Per 100 g, pork pau provides 234 kcal, 8.77 g protein, 8.3 g fat, and 31.12 g carbohydrate with 11.08 g sugar and only 1.0 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 369 mg/100 g, primarily from soy sauce and oyster sauce in the filling. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 16 per standard 80 g serving. Clinically, pork pau is a concern for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its high refined carbohydrate and sugar content with minimal fibre; for hypertension due to its sodium load and sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention; and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from pork and shortening plus trace trans fat. It is not halal-certified as it contains pork. Pork pau is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a morning snack and is culturally significant in Singaporean Chinese cuisine, with roots in Cantonese dim sum tradition.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a pork pau?
Is pork pau high in sodium?
What is pork pau made of?
How much carbohydrate in pork pau?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95 with exact match to the named food. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database class for steamed white-flour buns, documented as such.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid fractionation data for pork pau are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is carried from the input health_suitability caution note; this value should be verified against the HPB source record directly. saturated_fat_g of 2.8 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for steamed pork bun (USDA SR Legacy #18350 class) as a supporting reference since HPB does not report saturated fat separately for this item; this is an approximation and should be confirmed. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on steamed white-flour bun class in the University of Sydney GI Database and Foster-Powell et al. (2002) and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed GI tables. Pork pau is not halal-certified due to pork content. The input glycemic_index field carried gi_value: 0 with source 'Embedded GI Database' — this was rejected as unsupported and replaced with the analogous estimate documented above.
